Facts.text: Pope Formosus crowned Arnulf King of Carinthia and Holy Roman Emperor
Battle of Cassel; Robert I the Frisian defeats Arnulf III/I
Simon de Brion elected Pope Martinus IV
Girolamo Masci elected Pope Nicolas IV
Pope Boniface VIII issues papal bull (decree) instating a Jubilee Year, granting forgiveness of sins and debts for those who fulfill various conditions
King Robert II of Scotland (1371-90) becomes the first monarch of the House of Stewart
New Amsterdam granted a Jewish burial site

Battle of Toulon [Battle of Cape Sicié]: Spanish naval fleet supported by France's Levant fleet breaks through a two-year-old British blockade of Italy, allowing the Spanish to go on the offensive against Savoy
French troops conquer Brussels
Jacobite troops vacate Aberdeen
British House of Lords rules authors do not have perpetual copyright
1st US joint stock company (to make cloth) offers shares at 10 cents
Jews expelled from their settlements on the outskirts of Warsaw, Poland
1st US ship to trade with China, "Empress of China", sails from NY
The Adams–Onís Treaty between Spain and the United States is signed, ceding Florida to the US and defining the boundary between the US and New Spain
Russia and Britain establish the Alaska-Canada boundary
Russia and Persia sign the Peace of Turkmantsjai
Dutch garrison evacuates fort Du Bus New Guinea
Battle of Buena Vista begins between US and Mexican troops
1st meeting of Republican Party (Michigan)
Republican Party holds its first national meeting in Pittsburgh
Organized baseball played in San Francisco for 1st time between the Eagles and the Red Rovers
Shoe-making workers of Lynn, Massachusetts, strike successfully for higher wages
On a bet Edward Weston leaves Boston to walk to Lincoln's inauguration
-27] Battle at Dalton Georgia
2nd and last day of Battle of Okolona, Mississippi
Skirmish at Calfkiller Creek (Battle of Dug Hill), Tennessee
Battle of Wilmington, NC (Fort Anderson) occupied by Federals
Tennessee adopts a new constitution abolishing slavery
1st national convention of Prohibition Party (Columbus Ohio)
Johns Hopkins University opens
Greenback Labor Party forms (Toledo, Ohio)
The Serbian kingdom is refounded.
With 120 miles James Saunders wins NYC's 24 hour race & $100 prize
Union Labor Party organized in Cincinnati
“Father of American Golf” John Reid first demonstrates golf on a Yonkers cow pasture to friends
US President Cleveland signs bill to admit Dakotas, Montana & Washington state to the union
Manitoba Rugby Football Union forms
Black postmaster lynched, his wife & 3 daughters shot in Lake City, South Carolina
Battle at Wynne's Hill, South Africa (Boers vs British army)

The Hague Tribunal gives its decision in claims against Venezuela; it sets the sum to be paid by Venezuela and gives preferential treatment to the three powers that initiated the block - Britain, Germany, and Italy
The United Kingdom sells a meteorological station on the South Orkney Islands to Argentina; the islands are subsequently claimed by the United Kingdom in 1908.
Black evangelist William J Seymour arrives in Los Angeles, California
Leonid Andreyev's "Zhizn Cheloveka" premieres in St Petersburg
London's first cabs with taximeters begin operating
The Canadian Parliament resolves to maintain union with the British Empire, while controlling domestic fiscal affairs
J Vedrines makes 1st airplane flight over 100 mph-161.29 kph
Germany begins "unrestricted" submarine war
WWI: Germany sinks American Merchant ship Carib with mine, and torpedoes Norwegian ship Regin

The House-Grey Memorandum, drafted by US and Britain, states: 'Should the Allies accept [the American idea of a conference to end the war] and should Germany refuse it, the United States would "probably" enter the war against Germany'
German Navy torpedoes 7 Dutch ships
Germany claims Baltic states, Finland & Ukraine from Russia
Congress authorizes Grant Memorial $1 gold coin
London issues a unilateral declaration of Egyptian independence
1st successful chinchilla farm in US, in Los Angeles, California
US Transcontinental airmail service begins
ARC soccer team forms in Alphen on the Rhine
Baruch Spinosa's house of mourning opened as a museum
1st solo flight from England to Australia lands in Darwin, 15 ½ days after take off, piloted by Australian aviator Bert Hinkler
Purple Heart (the Badge of Military Merit) award reinstituted
Hungarian scholar and the founder of Tibetan studies Alexander Csoma de Kőrös declared a Bodhisattva (Buddhist saint) in Japan
Airplanes are no longer permitted to fly over the White House
Construction on Ypenburg Netherlands airport begins
Finnish troops vacate Koivisto island
German air force sinks 2 German destroyers, killing 578
Arthur "Bomber" Harris becomes British Air Marshal
German assault on El Agheila Libya
IG Farben company decides to build its Buna-Werke (rubber factory) adjacent to Auschwitz Concentration Camp to have easy access to slave labor and safe cover from Allied bombing
Nazi police raid Amsterdam and round up 429 young Jews for deportation to be sent to Buchenwald and Mauthausen concentration camps
Paul Creston's 1st Symphony premieres
Roy Harris' "Ballad of a Railroad Man" premieres
3 members of White Rose student anti-Nazi resistance group are executed in Munich, Germany
World War II: US Army Air Forces accidentally bomb Dutch town of Nijmegen, around 800 civilians die
Arab League forms (Cairo)
British troops take Ramree Island, Burma
Canadian 3rd Division occupies Moyland
Arabs bomb attack in Jerusalem, 50 die
Wallace Brockway and Herbert Weinstock's revised and expanded edition of reference book "Men of Music: Their Lives, Times and Achievements" is published
German husband and wife team Ria and Paul Falk win the mixed pairs gold medal at the Oslo Winter Olympics; defeat American siblings, Karol and Peter Kennedy
Germany takes the Olympic bobsleigh double with gold in the 4-man event at the Oslo Winter Games; Adreas Ostler and Lorenz Niebert score their 2nd gold medals after winning the 2-man a week earlier
British aircraft carrier HMS Ark Royal sets sail
1st English soccer match at Kunstlicht: Portsmouth vs Newcastle United
Jockey Ted Atkinson, 3,500th win
Walter O'Malley says Dodgers may play 10 exhibitions in California in 1958
Australian swimmer Jon Konrads sets 6 world records in 2 days
Egypt & Syria form United Arab Republic (UAR)
Indonesian air force bombs Padang, Sumatra and Menado, Celebes
Inaugural Daytona 500: Lee Petty and Johnny Beauchamp cross finish line side by side; Beauchamp declared unofficial winner; ruling overturned after 3 days by NASCAR founder Bill France Sr
German skier Georg Thoma becomes the first non-Nordic athlete to win the Nordic combined event at the Squaw Valley Winter Olympics
The Beatles begin their own music publishing company (Northern Songs)
The Beatles arrive back in England after their successful first US visit
Rodgers & Hammerstein's television musical "Cinderella", starring Lesley Ann Warren and Stuart Damon, with Pat Carroll and Celeste Holm, premieres on CBS-TV
USSR launches Kosmos 57 into earth orbit (Voskhod Test)
Soviets launch Kosmos 110 with Veterok & Ugolek, 1st 2-dog crew
25,000 US and South Vietnamese troops launch Operation Junction City against the Viet Cong. Largest US airborne assault since World War II.
Barbara Garson's "MacBird" premieres in NYC
Sling-shot goal post & 6' wide border around field are standard in NFL
Barbara Jo Rubin becomes 1st female jockey to win at an American parimutuel race at a major US track aboard Cohesian at Charles Town, West Virginia

The Beatles begin recording what becomes their "Abbey Road" album at EMI Recording Studio at 3 Abbey Road in Westminster, London, England; the studio was later renamed in honor of their achievements there
Khalifa bin Hamad Al Thani becomes Emir & Prime Minister of Qatar
Red Wings rookie Henry Boucha scores in his first NHL Game in 5-4 win over Toronto Maple Leafs at Olympias Arena in Detroit, Michigan
The Official IRA bombs Aldershot military barracks, the headquarters of the British Parachute Regiment, killing seven people; thought to be in retaliation for Bloody Sunday.
"Turkish Delight" Holland's most successful film, directed by Paul Verhoeven and starring Rutger Hauer is released
US and China agree to establish liaison offices in Beijing and Washington, D.C.
Ethiopian police shoot at demonstrators
2 tankers with propane gas explode killing 15 at Waverly, Tenn
Cleveland Metroparks Zoo's Primate & Cat Building is dedicated (Cleveland, Ohio)
St Lucia gains independence from Britain
Afghanistan declares martial law
Aleksandr Tikhonov of the Soviet Union earns his 4th straight gold medal as part of the 4 x 7.5k biathlon relay team at the Lake Placid Winter Olympics
Soviet biathlete Anatoly Alyabyev wins his 2nd gold medal of the Lake Placid Winter Olympics as part of the 4 x 7.5k relay team; also wins individual gold
Swedish World Cup and World champion Ingemar Stenmark wins his 2nd gold medal of the Lake Placid Winter Olympics, taking out the slalom; follows-on from giant slalom success
NYC Mayor Koch announces he will run for NY governor (unsuccessful)
Hindus kill 3000 Muslims in Assam, India
Vladimir Salnikov (USSR) sets 1500m free style swimming record
Start of the People Power Revolution in the Philippines
Bruno Marie-Rose runs indoor world record 200m (20.36 sec)
Finish ministry of Public health installs sex vacation to thwart stress
NY Lotto pays $26.9 million to one winner (#s are 1-5-12-19-44-50)
Pepsi-Cola airs 1st Spanish commercial on network TV during the Grammy Awards broadcast on CBS
US authors demonstrate against Iranian death threats against Salman Rushdee, author of "The Satanic Verses"
1st day India v NZ cricket at Auckland NZ 5-78 at lunch, 9-387 stumps
"Park Your Car in Harvard Yard" closes at Music Box NYC
Barry Diller resigns as CEO of Fox
Vinod Kambli scores 224 v England at Bombay, 411 balls, 23 fours
Algiers police kill at least 99 prison rioters
Steve Fossett completes 1st air balloon over Pacific Ocean (9600 km)
Spaces shuttle Columbia 19 (STS 75) launches into orbit
Scientists at the Roslin Institute in Scotland announce they have cloned an adult mammal, producing Dolly the sheep - born 5 July 1996 (d. 2003)
Petr Svoboda scores the winner as the Czech Republic claims its first ever Olympic ice hockey gold medal with a 1-0 victory over Russia in Nagano, Japan
XVIII Winter Olympic Games close in Nagano Japan
Angolan revolutionary politician and military leader of UNITA, Jonas Savimbi is killed in a military ambush in the province of Moxico
Pakistani pace bowler Shoaib Akhtar bowls the fastest ball recorded in cricket history at 100.2 mph (161.3 km/h)
At least six men stage Britain's biggest robbery ever, stealing £53m (about $92.5 million or 78€ million) from a Securitas depot in Tonbridge, Kent
Taiwan's economy exits from the recession with 9.22% growth in the last quarter of 2009 after increased demand from China and other key markets in the region
An earthquake measuring 6.3 in magnitude strikes Christchurch, New Zealand, killing 181 people
Train crash in Buenos Aires, Argentina, kills 50 and injures hundreds
13 Chadian soldiers and 65 Muslim insurgents are killed in conflict in Northern Mali
29 people are killed and 150 are injured by 3 Syrian army missiles in Aleppo
The European Commission forecast for 2013 expects growth of 0.1% across the 27 members of the EU but a contraction of 0.3% in the Eurozone economy
The UK's AAA credit rating is downgraded by Moody's Investors Service to AA+; the agency expects growth to "remain sluggish over the next few years"
After winning the men's parallel giant slalom at the Sochi Winter Olympics, Russian snowboarder Vic Wild wins his 2nd gold in the parallel slalom
At age 34, Mario Matt of Austria becomes the oldest champion in Olympic alpine skiing history with the slalom gold medal at Sochi
Dutch speed skaters Jorien ter Mors (1,500m champion) and Ireen Wüst (3,000m winner) each claim their 2nd gold medal of the Sochi Winter Olympics as part of the women's pursuit team
Matteo Renzi becomes Prime Minister of Italy
Norway sweeps the medals in the women's 30k cross country skiing at the Sochi Winter Olympics; Marit Bjørgen wins her 6th career gold ahead of teammates Therese Johaug and Kristin Størmer Steira
Snowboard Olympic parallel special slalom event debuts and is run for the only time at the Sochi Winter Games; Russian Vic Wild and Julia Dujmovits of Canada win unique gold medals
57th Daytona 500: Joey Logano wins event for 2nd time after taking lead with 10 laps to go; is ahead of Kevin Harvick before caution flag on last lap ends race in his favour
10 million people are without water in Delhi after caste protests in Jat sabotage the Munak water canal
Apple's new campus is named Apple Park in Culpertino, California, nicknamed 'the spaceship', designed with Foster + Partners, the first of 12,000 employees to move in, in April
Discovery of 7 Earth-sized planets orbiting star Trappist-1 announced in Journal "Nature" - raises possibility of alien life
Chinese short track speed skater Wu Dajing beats men's 500m world record twice en route to winning the gold medal at the Pyeongchang Winter Olympics; 2nd man in history under 40 seconds (39.584s)
German individual Nordic combined 10k large hill champion Johannes Rydzek and normal hill winner Eric Frenzel win their 2nd gold medals of the Pyeongchang Winter Olympics in the 4 x 5k team event
Neanderthals, not humans, were the first artists on Earth, producing red cave paintings 65,000 years ago in Spain, according to new research published in "Science"
US wins Olympic women's ice hockey gold medal for the first time in 20 years with a 4-3 shootout victory over Canada in Pyeongchang; scores tied 3-3 after overtime
Women's big air snowboarding event makes Olympic debut at Pyeongchang; Austria's Anna Gasser is inaugural gold medallist
Actor Jussie Smollett suspended from US TV show "Empire" after revealed that actor made false claims about racist and homophobic attack
Musician Peter Frampton announces he has a degenerative muscular disease and his next tour will be his last
Influential French dance duo Daft Punk announce their split after 28 years
Three white men convicted of killing black jogger Ahmaud Arbery found guilty of federal hate crimes, in Georgia
US PresidentJoe Bidenannounces new sanctions against Russia, saying its latest moves in Ukraine amount to "the beginning of a Russian invasion"
US Women soccer players settle their lawsuit with US soccer federation for $24 million and a promise to equalise pay
Astronomers reveal Webb telescope data is upending existing theories of how early galaxies were formed, after finding six massive galaxies 100x larger than expected soon after the Big Bang
Japan’s benchmark stock exchange, the Nikkei average reaches its highest-ever level at 39,098, breaking its 1989 record
Odysseus is the first private spacecraft to land on the moon, albeit on its side, the first US moon landing since 1972
First official Roman edict for persecution of Christians issued by EmperorDiocletianat Nicomedia, ordering all churches to be closed and scriptures burnt
English KingHenry Vlays the foundation stone for Syon Abbey for nuns of the Bridgettine Order. Became one of the wealthiest abbeys in England.
French KingCharles VIIIenters Naples to claim crown
Galileo Galilei's book "Dialogue Concerning the Two Chief World Systems" is published comparing the Copernican and Ptolemaic systems and whether the Earth orbits the sun
St. Peter's Baldachin, the cathedral's sculptural centerpiece, byGian Lorenzo Berniniis inaugurated by Pope Urban VIII in Rome
Royalist troops of QueenMaria IIof Portugal put down a revolutionary insurrection
Dion Boucicault's play "Jessie Brown" premieres in NYC
PeterTchaikovsky's 4th Symphony in F premieres at a Russian Musical Society concert in Moscow [February 10 O.S.]
Malcolm Campbellsets a world land speed record of 272.46 mph driving his famous Blue Bird car at Daytona Beach, Florida

"It Happened One Night" directed byFrank Capraand starringClark GableandClaudette Colbertopens at NY's Radio City Music Hall (Academy Awards Best Picture, Director, Actor, Actress and Screenplay 1935)
André Malrauxand Édouard Corniglion-Molinier set out to find the lost capital of the Queen of Sheba, as mentioned in the Old Testament
"The Little Colonel" premieres starringShirley Temple,Lionel BarrymoreandBill Robinson, featuring famous stair dance with Hollywood's first interracial dance couple
Sonja Henieof Norway follows up her Winter Olympics victory with an incredible 10th consecutive women’s World Championship figure skating gold medal in Paris, France
Netherlands recognizesFrancisco Franco's dictatorial regime in Spain
Plane crash in the Tagus River, Lisbon, Portugal kills 23 with 15 survivors including singerJane Froman
Philadelphia centerWilt Chamberlainsets NBA record with 34 free-throw attempts (converts 19) in Warriors 139-121 win over St. Louis Hawks at Philadelphia Civic Center
Rock groupGenesisrelease their 1st record "Silent Sun"
12th Daytona 500: Pete Hamilton wins by just 3 car lengths overDavid Pearson, after passing him with 9 laps to go
US PresidentRichard Nixon, meets with Chinese PremierZhou Enlaiin Beijing
Billy Martinnamed manager of Oakland A's
"Miracle on Ice": US ice hockey team beats heavily favoured Soviet Union, 4-3 at Lake Placid in one of the biggest upsets in Olympic history; Americans go on to win gold medal
Slovak brothers Anton &Peter Šťastnýscore NHL rookie record 8 points each in Quebec Nordiques, 11-7 win over Washington Capitals at Capital Centre
Harold Washingtonwins Chicago's Democratic mayoral primary
Bonnie Blairskates world record 500m (39.10 sec)
31st Grammy Awards: "Don't Worry Be Happy" by Bobby McFerrin; "Faith"byGeorge Michael; andTracy Chapmanwin
UK physicistStephen Hawkingcalls the proposed missile defense system known as Star Wars a "deliberate fraud"
Test Cricket debut ofSanath Jayasuriya, vs NZ at Hamilton
Barbra Streisandsurprises cast members in a cameo appearance at the end of a "Coffee Talk" sketch on "Saturday Night Live"
Marco van Bastensigns a four-year contract with Ajax, starting from 1 July
81st Academy Awards: "Slumdog Millionaire",Sean PennandKate Winsletwin.Heath Ledgerposthumously wins Best Supporting Actor for his performance as the Joker in "The Dark Knight".
Viktor Yanukovychis ousted as President of Ukraine by the parliament following the Euromaidan revolution

87th Academy Awards: "Best Picture - "Birdman", Best Director, and Best Screenplay - Alejandro González Iñárritu ("Birdman:"), Best Actor -Eddie Redmayne– ("The Theory of Everything"), Best Actress -Julianne Moore("Still Alice")
Alex Pucciowins the Hueco Tanks 'Rock Rodeo'
Jay-Zbecomes the first rapper inducted into the Songwriters Hall of Fame alongside Max Martin, Jimmy Jam, and Terry Lewis
US PresidentDonald TrumpoverturnsObamadirective on Transgender rights to use toilets
Malaysian Prime MinisterNajib Razakstirs up controversy by declaring he prefers quinoa to rice, the national dish
Robert Kraft, owner of NFL team the New England Patriots, charged with soliciting prostitution, as part of human-trafficking sting operation in Jupiter, Florida
Portuguese superstarCristiano Ronaldomarks his 1,000th senior appearance by scoring in Juventus' 2-1 Serie A win over SPAL; 725 career goals in 836 club games and 164 internationals

US death toll from COVID-19 passes 500,000, higher than US deaths in World War I, World War II, and the Vietnam War combined.Joe BidenandKamala Harrishold a candle-lighting ceremony outside the White House and say "we must not grow numb to the sorrow".
The Last Invasion of Britain, launched by the French during the Revolutionary Wars, begins near Fishguard, Wales
First "Woolworth's Great Five Cent Store" opened byFrank Winfield Woolworthin Utica, New York. It fails almost immediately.
Great White Fleet, 1st US fleet to circle the globe, returns to Virginia
World War II: President Franklin Roosevelt orders GeneralDouglas MacArthurout of the Philippines as American defenses collapse
Wife of drug cartel boss "El Chapo" Emma Coronel Aispuro arrested in the US on drug trafficking charges and conspiring to free her husband from prison
Rashi, French rabbi who wrote commentaries on the Talmud and Hebrew Bible, born in Troyes, France (d. 1105)
Charles VII, King of France (1422-61) who drove the English out from Northern France, born in Paris France (d. 1461)
Ladislaus V ("the Posthumus"), Árpád dynasty and King of Bohemia (1453-57), King of Hungary and Croatia (1440-44), and Duke of Austria (1440-57), born in Komárom, Kingdom of Hungary (d. 1457)
Rodolfo Pio da Carpi, Italian Cardinal and humanist, born in Carpi, Duchy of Modena and Reggio (d. 1564)
Tahmasp I, shah of Iran (1524-76) and author (Tazkire-i Shah), born in Shāhābād, Safavid Iran (d. 1576)
Santino Garsi da Parma, Italian lutenist and composer, born in Parma, Papal States (d. 1604)
Gemignano Capilupi, Italian choral music composer, baptized in Modena, Duchy of Modena and Reggio, House of Este (d. 1616)
George Digby, 2nd Earl of Bristol, English statesman, baptized in Madrid, Spain (d. 1677)
Peter van Schooten, Dutch mathematician and fort architect, born in Leiden, Netherlands (d. 1679)
William Pulteney, English politician (1st Earl of Bath, shortest-ever British Prime Minister for 2 days in 1746 disputed), born in London, England (d. 1764)
Peter Arctedi, Swedish biologist ("father of ichthyology" study of fish), born in Anundsjö, Sweden (d. 1735)
Louis-Georges de Bréquigny, French historian, born in Granville, France (d. 1795)
João de Sousa Carvalho, Portuguese composer and music teacher to the royal family, born in Estremoz, Portugal (d. 1798)
Johann Nikolaus Forkel, German musicologist (1st biography of Johann Sebastian Bach), born in Meeder, Coburg (d. 1818)
Georg Friedrich von Martens, German diplomat and jurist, born in Hamburg (d. 1821)
Erik Tulindberg, Finland's first known classical composer, born in Vähäkyrö, Finland (d. 1814)
Alexander Campbell, Scottish composer and writer (associate of Walter Scott), born in Tombea, Scotland (d. 1824)
Jan August Vitasek, Bohemian composer working in Prague, born in Hořín, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1839)
Joseph Lipavsky, Bohemian composer and pianist, born in Hohenmauth, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1810)
Karl Jacob Wagner, German composer, born in Darmstadt, Landgraviate of Hesse-Darmstadt, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1822)
Matthijs Ignatius van Bree, Belgian painter of historical subjects, born in Antwerp (d. 1839)
Rembrandt Peale, American portrait and historical painter (Margaret Irvine Miller ,1805; Court of Death, 1820), and museum keeper (Baltimore), born in Bucks County, Pennsylvania (d. 1860)
Joachim Nicolas Eggert, Swedish violinist and composer, born in Ginst, Rügen Island, Swedish Pomerania (d. 1813)
Jean Charles Athanase Peltier, French Physicist (Peltier effect 1834), born in Ham, France (d. 1845)
Arthur Schopenhauer, German philosopher ("The World as Will and Representation") known for his philosophical pessimism, born in Danzig (Gdańsk), Poland (d. 1860)
Adolphe Quetelet, Belgian astronomer, mathematician and sociologist (body mass index scale), born in Ghent, French Republic (d. 1874)
Alexis Bachelot, French missionary, led first permanent Catholic mission to Hawaii, born in Saint-Cyr-la-Rosière, France (d. 1837)
Charles Mynn Thruston, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Lexington, Kentucky (d. 1873)
Józef Kremer, Polish philosopher, art historian and psychologist, born in Kraków, Poland (d. 1875)
Holger Simon Paulli, Danish conductor and composer, born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1891)
Oskar Kolberg, Polish ethnographer, folklorist, and composer (Lud), born in Przysucha, Poland (d. 1890)
Carl Wilhelm Borchardt, German mathematician who worked in analysis, born in Rudersdorf, Prussia (d. 1880)
Niels Wilhelm Gade, Danish violinist, organist, composer, and conductor, born in Copenhagen (d. 1890)
Bernardo Calvo Puig, Spanish composer, born in Vic, Spain (d. 1880)
James Russell Lowell, American poet, critic and diplomat (Biglow Papers), born in Cambrisge, Massachusetts (d. 1891)
Adolf Kussmaul, German physician (stomach pump, Kuszmaul disease), born in Graben (d. 1902)
Pierre Janssen, French astronomer, discovered helium, born in Paris, France (d. 1907)
Jean Baptiste Salpointe, second Archbishop of Santa Fe, born in Saint-Maurice de Pionsat, France (d. 1898)
James Barnet Fry, American historian and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Carrollton, Illinois (d. 1894)
Robert Alexander Cameron, American soldier and newspaper publisher (Union Army), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1894)
Josef Foerster, Czech composer and music teacher, born in Osenice, Bohemia, Austro-Hungarian Empire (d. 1907)

Albert Zabel, German-Russian prodigy harpist (Berlin Opera, 1848-55; Russian Imperial Ballet, 1855-1910), pedagogue (St. Petersburg Conservatory, 1962-1910), and composer (La Source), born in Berlin, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1910)
Eduard Wachmann, Romanian composer, conductor and university professor, born in Bucharest, Wallachia (d. 1908)
Francis Pharcellus Church, American editor and publisher (The New York Sun - "Yes, Virginia, There Is A Santa Claus"), born in Rochester, New York (d. 1906)
Octavius Catto, American educator (Institute for Colored Youth, 1859-71), civil rights activist, and baseball player (Philadelphia Pythians, 1865-71), born in Charleston, South Carolina (d. 1871)
August Bebel, German socialist politician (one of the founders of the Social Democratic Worker's Party of Germany), born in Deutz (d. 1913)
Leon Vanderkindere, Belgian historian and mayor (Ukkel), born in Sint-Jans-Molenbeek (d. 1906)
Aldredo de Escragnolle Taunay, Brazilian writer (Inocencia), musician and politician, born in Rio Janeiro, Brazil (d. 1899)
Kazamierz Julian Kratzer, Polish composer, born in Kraków, Poland (d. 1890)
Nikolay Yakovlevich Sonin, Russian mathematician known for the Sonine formula, born in Tula, Russia (d. 1915)
Isaac Rice, German-American businessman (namesake of Columbia Univ's Rice stadium, Electric Boat Company), born in Wachenheim, Bavaria (d. 1915)
Pieter Klazes Pel, Dutch physician (Pel-Ebstein fever), born in Drachten, Smallingerland, Netherlands (d. 1919)
Heinrich Hertz, German physicist, 1st to broadcast & receive radio waves, born in Hamburg, German Confederation (d. 1894)
Joey Palmer, Australian cricket medium pace/spin bowler (17 Tests, 78 wickets, BB 7/65; Victoria CA), born in Corowa, Australia (d. 1910)
Karen Hulda Garborg, Norwegian playwright (Mot Solen/Eli), born near Hamar, Norway (d. 1934)
Jules Renard, French writer (Poil de Carotte), born in Châlons-du-Maine, France (d. 1910)
Otto Modersohn, German landscape painter (Worpswede artist colony), born in Soest (d. 1943)
Henri Polak, Dutch labor leader (General Diamond Workers' Union), journalist, and Social-Democrat politician (Member of Parliament), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1943)
Bill Klem, American Baseball HOF umpire (record 18 x World Series), born in Rochester, New York (d. 1951)
Yme Schuitmaker, Frisian-Dutch potato merchant, actor, and playwright (Maerteblom (Snowdrop); Lysbet), born in Frjentsjer, Friesland, Dutch Republic (d. 1961)
Walter Ritz, Swiss physicist, known for the Rydberg–Ritz combination principle, born in Sion, Switzerland (d. 1909)
Norman Lindsay, Australian artist and novelist (The Magic Pudding), born in Creswick Victoria (d. 1969)[1]
Frigyes Riesz, Hungarian mathematician, helped found functional analysis, born in Győr, Austria-Hungary (d. 1956)
James Reese Europe, American ragtime and early jazz arranger, composer, and bandleader in New York, and with the military in France during WWI, born in Mobile, Alabama (d. 1919)[1][2]
Joseph B. Ely, American politician, 52nd Governor of Massachusetts who opposed Roosevelt, born in Westfield Massachusetts (d. 1956)
Eric Gill, English sculptor, engraver and typographer (Perpetua, Gill sans), born in Brighton, England (d. 1940)
Alfred Wikenhauser, German RC exegetist (John-Apokalyps), born in Engen, Germany (d. 1960)
Jaroslav Kocián, Czech composer and teacher, born in Ústí nad Orlicí, Austria (d. 1950)
Marguerite Clark, American silent film actress (Snow White), born in Avondale, Cincinnati (d. 1940)
Hugo Ball, German playwright and social critic (co-founder Flametti, Dada), born in Pirmasens, Germany (d. 1927)
Owen Brewster, American politician, 54th Governor of Maine (1925-29), Senator and Representative, born in Dexter, Maine, (d. 1961)
Olave Baden-Powell, English founder of the Girl Guides, born in  Chesterfield, England (d. 1977)
Robin G. Collingwood, English philosopher. historian ("The Principles of Art"; "The Idea of History") and archaeologist (Roman Britain), born in Cartmel Fell, England (d. 1943)
Jan Wils, Dutch architect (Amsterdam's Olympic Stadium), born in Alkmaar, Netherlands (d. 1972)
Vlas Chubar, Ukrainian Bolshevik revolutionary and Soviet politician, born in Fedorivka, Yekaterinoslav Governorate, Imperial Russia (d. 1939)
David Dubinsky, Belarusian-born American labor leader (Freedom Award, 1969 Medal of Freedom), born in Brest, Russian Empire (d. 1982)
Edna St Vincent Millay, American poet, dramatist and feminist (Harp Weaver-Pulitzer Prize), born in Rockland, Maine (d. 1950)
Alexander Spitzmüller, Austrian composer, educator, radio broadcaster, and music critic, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1962)
Victor Raúl Haya de la Torre, Peruvian politician and author (founder of Peruvian Aprista Party), born in Trujillo, Peru (d. 1979)
Edvin Wide, Swedish athlete (Olympic silver 10,000m 1924; 4 x bronze 1920, 24, 28; WR 2,000/3,000m), born in Kimito, Finland (d. 1996)
Enid Markey, American actress, 1st to play Tarzan's Jane, born in Dillon, Colorado (d. 1981)
Nacio Herb Brown, American composer (Singin' In The Rain), born in Deming, New Mexico (d. 1964)
Paul Van Ostaijen, Belgian-Dutch poet, writer and critic (Occupied City), born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1928)
Bob Ysaguirre, American jazz double bass and tuba player (Don Redman Orchestra), born in Belize, British Honduras (d. 1982)
Karol Świerczewski, Polish general who served the Soviet Union and Republican Spain, born in Warsaw, Poland (d. 1947)
Anton de Kom, Surinamese worker's union leader and resistance fighter, born in Paramaribo, Suriname (d. 1945)
Dechko Uzunov, Bulgarian painter, born in Kazanluk, Bulgaria (d. 1986)
